name: B.S. in Construction Engineering Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan builds a clear, well-ordered construction-engineering spine: MATH 138 -> 238 and PHYS 102 -> 103 feed the mechanics chain MET 235 (Statics) -> MET 237 (Strength of Materials) -> CET 233 (Structural Analysis, depth 6) -> CET 331 (Structural Systems, depth 7) -> CET 435 (Temporary Structures, depth 8), while CET 313/314 procedures, 317 computing, 322 codes, 341 soils, 411 estimating, 415/416 project management and senior project, 421 contracts, 423 safety and 431 testing round out the professional core. Progression is smooth (depths rise 0->8) and the senior year integrates management with design.
dimensions.suggestion: Sequence the several undifferentiated 'None' technical-elective/GER slots (notably the Second Year 2nd Semester term, where four of five items are unlabeled) into named clusters so the plan reads as intentional depth rather than open choice.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy with a deepest chain of 8 (CET 435 <- CET 331 <- CET 233 <- MET 237) and sensible compound conditions (MET 237 requires '(MET 235 or MECH 234 or MECH 235) and (MATH 138 or MATH 111)'). All 5 dangling references in missingCoursesSample (GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A, PHYS 103A, SET 200A) are gen-ed placeholders and physics/geomatics lab codes, not genuine breaks in the CET sequence.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the placeholder/lab catalog entries (PHYS 102A/103A, SET 200A) and the GER 200/300 placeholders so metrics show zero dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET/ATMAE-style educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. For an accredited-track engineering-technology B.S. this absence is a genuine catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program student outcomes (structural/construction analysis, estimating and scheduling, professional/ethical responsibility, communication) to support accreditation reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 (creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and every term is loaded at 15 credits. The distribution transparently balances a math/physics base, a mechanics/structures core, the CET professional sequence, and gen-ed/electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line credit-summary table (math-science vs. CET core vs. GER vs. technical electives) so the balanced split is visible without reading all eight terms.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and the required CET courses are legible with rich descriptions, but the plan carries a null-code FYS seminar row at 0 credits and numerous unlabeled 'None' rows for GER/technical-elective slots (the Second Year 2nd Semester term shows only CET 233 with four blank rows), which reads as under-specified.
dimensions.suggestion: Give every requirement row a course code or an explicit named placeholder (e.g., 'Humanities GER 200-level', 'Technical Elective') so no plan row is blank.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core reflects sound construction-engineering fundamentals: estimating (CET 411), project management (CET 415/416), codes (CET 322), safety/OSHA (CET 423), environmental science (CET 413), structural design (CET 233/331/435) and testing (CET 431). The main currency gaps are Building Information Modeling and digital delivery (CET 317 stops at general 'construction computing' and MET 103/105 at AutoCAD), and there is no dedicated sustainable/green-construction course.
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize CET 317 toward BIM/VDC (Revit, model-based coordination and 4D/5D scheduling) and add a sustainable-construction/LEED elective.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 32 sampled courses carry descriptions and most are specific and technical (CET 233 on steel and reinforced-concrete design, CET 322 on the NJ Uniform Construction Code and CSI format, CET 341 on soil mechanics and foundations). A few are terse (CET 341) and MATH 138 ends with a stray '298 Jordan' artifact, but overall description quality is strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated explicitly with consistent grade conditions and sensible compound logic (CET 331 <- 'CET 233 and MATH 238'; CET 411 <- 'CET 313, CET 314, CET 317'). The only detractors are the placeholder/lab dangling references and a couple of restriction notes embedded in descriptions rather than the prerequisite field.

missingTopics.rationale: CET 317 Construction Computing and MET 103/105 stop at AutoCAD; modern construction engineering is model-centric, so a Revit/BIM coordination course is expected.
CET 413 Environmental Science touches environmental topics but there is no dedicated course on energy modeling, LEED, or low-carbon materials.

missingTopics.topic: Lean construction and digital project controls
missingTopics.rationale: CET 415 project management and CET 411 estimating are traditional; Last Planner, lean scheduling and 4D/5D BIM-based controls are absent.
CET 341 Soils and Earthwork is the single geotechnical course; deep-foundation design is underweighted for a construction-engineering track.

missingTopics.topic: Reality capture and construction data analytics
missingTopics.rationale: SET 200 Geomatics is present but drones/photogrammetry and jobsite-data analytics are not integrated into the CET sequence.

emergingTrends.rationale: The CET 317/411/415 cluster is the natural home for model-based scheduling and cost, positioning graduates for BIM-driven delivery.
Offsite/prefab methods are reshaping the field but appear nowhere in the CET procedures courses (313/314).

emergingTrends.trend: Construction robotics and drones for site monitoring
emergingTrends.rationale: Automated layout and drone progress-tracking are becoming standard and connect to SET 200 geomatics content.
Embodied-carbon accounting for materials is an emerging owner requirement that CET 413 could extend.
CET 411 estimating is ripe for modernization toward data-driven, AI-assisted quantity takeoff and productivity analytics.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / student outcomes, which are absent and are needed for accreditation reporting.
Modernize CET 317 toward BIM/VDC and add a sustainable-construction/LEED elective to close the two biggest currency gaps.
Introduce lean-construction and 4D/5D digital project-controls content into CET 411/415.
Label the blank 'None' elective/GER plan rows and add a credit-summary table for readability.
Resolve the placeholder dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A/103A, SET 200A).
